competition.narrative: Competition is active and multi-faceted. The primary current threat is DO NOTHING (SAP Ariba status quo). On the 10 Jun intro call, Óscar stated that SuKarne is already on SAP S/4HANA (recently migrated) and SAP Ariba, with contracts, guided sourcing, and supplier discovery modules enabled but underutilized due to lack of training and focus: 'tenemos Ariba... pero no sabemos usarlo, es falta de entrenamiento' (we have Ariba but don't know how to use it, it's lack of training). He also noted: 'creo que está bien, es que estamos, no estamos enfocados, estamos atrapados en el ' (Ariba is fine, we are just not focused, we are trapped in transactional work). This is the classic do-nothing competitor: the buyer owns a capable incumbent but is underutilizing it, so the wedge is not 'replace Ariba' but 'complement Ariba with Zycus to automate the intake/status-query problem Ariba does not solve, and unlock Ariba's advanced modules by giving users a Google Chat interface they will actually adopt'. The second threat is SAP JULES (SAP's procurement AI agent), which Óscar mentioned he declined as an early adopter but requested an H2 2026 invite. If Zycus does not close or advance to pilot by Q4 2026, SAP Jules becomes a live alternative when SAP re-engages in H2 2026. The third threat is a SERVICES-LED SAP PARTNER alternative. Óscar stated on the 10 Jun call: 'Considering engaging an SAP partner to handle SAP work and Google-based automations for purchase automation project'. This is a medium threat: if Óscar concludes his automation project can be delivered via an SAP partner's custom Google Workspace integration (lower cost, familiar SAP relationship), Zycus loses. The wedge is that Zycus offers a proven, enterprise-grade, SAP-native platform with Merlin agentic AI purpose-built for procurement, whereas an SAP partner delivers custom services with longer timelines, higher risk, and no ongoing product innovation. How we win: (1) Position Zycus as COMPLEMENTARY to SAP/Ariba, not competitive (de-risk the post-migration integration anxiety). (2) Anchor on the Google Chat + Merlin interface as the adoption unlock (Ariba failed due to complexity; Zycus succeeds via familiar UX). (3) Deliver a quick-win pilot on intake automation and status-query reduction (the 40% time-waste problem) before SAP Jules or an SAP partner can mobilize. (4) Multi-thread to the CIO (Huascar Garcia) to validate native SAP integration and de-risk IT concerns, and to Finance to build the EB-ready ROI case.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed, specific, and owned. Óscar (Deputy Director of Procurement and Strategic Sourcing) articulated three operational pains on the 10 Jun intro call: (1) Procurement is stuck in transactional mode (invoice-to-pay, PO-to-pay) and lacks strategic capability (category management, strategic sourcing, demand planning). He stated: 'hoy somos lo que le llaman transaccionales, invoice to pay and purchase to pay, nada más, no tenemos sourcing' (today we are transactional, invoice-to-pay and purchase-to-pay only, we have no sourcing). (2) High volume of repetitive, low-value work: 'más de cuarenta por ciento del tiempo se van a hacer cosas repetitivas, como haciendo seguimientos, a veces preguntas de los equipos de cómo entrar una requisición o de qué pasó con el pedido' (more than 40% of time goes to repetitive tasks like follow-ups and user questions about requisition entry or order status). The AI-generated note summarized this as 'High volume of repetitive status queries from users indicating lack of visibility and of automated tracking'. (3) Data and system maintenance gaps: 'sistemas diseñados earlier no fueron actualizados as company grew, causing coverage gaps and visibility loss' (systems designed earlier were not updated as company grew, causing coverage gaps and visibility loss). The pain owner is Óscar, who is the Deputy Director of Procurement and reports to the CPO or directly to the CFO (org chart not confirmed). The pain is operational (time waste, lack of visibility) and strategic (cannot execute category management or strategic sourcing without better tools and data). The compelling event is implicit: Óscar is running an 'automation of purchases' project to modernize procurement, and he committed to present the project scope on the 30 Jun call. If Zycus can align its Merlin intake automation and spend analytics to his project, the pain becomes the business case.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are emerging but not yet formalized. On the 10 Jun intro call, Óscar articulated his transformation priorities: (1) move procurement from transactional to a center of excellence, (2) implement category management, (3) implement spend management and analytics, (4) implement strategic sourcing, and (5) automate repetitive work (status queries, requisition guidance). He explicitly stated his objective: 'todo lo que me pueda ayudar para llevar al área de la situación actual a una a un área de compras moderna, con de source to pay'. He also flagged that SAP Ariba is underutilized due to lack of training and focus, and that the recent S/4HANA migration left integration/maturity issues. Implicitly, the evaluation criteria are: (1) ease of use (no steep learning curve like Ariba; user adoption via familiar interface like Google Chat was positively received), (2) native SAP integration (to avoid another painful integration after the S/4 migration), (3) automation of intake and status tracking (the 40% time-waste problem), (4) spend visibility and analytics (to enable category management without requiring full master-data cleanup upfront), and (5) AI/automation fit with SuKarne's Google Workspace + Gemini environment (Óscar is running Gemini training and was receptive to Merlin agents in Google Chat). However, no formal RFP, BRD, or scorecard has been introduced. The 30 Jun automation project presentation will clarify the specific processes Óscar wants to automate, which will define the must-have vs nice-to-have criteria. Zycus positioning must anchor on: Google Chat interface (no Ariba-style learning curve), native SAP connectivity (de-risk integration), immediate automation value (status queries, intake orchestration) without requiring master-data cleanup first, and Merlin agentic AI as a natural extension of SuKarne's Gemini investment.

flags: Single-threaded at 16 days: Óscar Felix Chaidez is the only engaged buyer contact (zero buyer-side contact roles on the opportunity). For a $150k, 12-month enterprise deal, multi-threading is essential. The immediate move is to ask Óscar on the 30 Jun call who else should see the solution (IT/CIO for integration and Google Workspace alignment, Finance/CFO for budget approval, Procurement leads in Nicaragua/Brazil if this is a multi-country rollout) and secure introductions. If Óscar is the sole advocate and he leaves or deprioritizes, the deal dies.

Economic Buyer and approval process unmapped: No budget authority, pricing conversation, or approval chain has been discussed. SuKarne is a large, family-owned enterprise; the Economic Buyer is likely CFO-level or the second-generation owner/CEO. The next gate after scoping the automation project (30 Jun) is to map the Economic Buyer, understand the approval process for a $150k investment, and secure an executive sponsor. Without this, the deal can stall at the commercial stage even if the champion is strong.

Differentiate fast against SAP Ariba and establish Merlin as the automation standard before Jules re-engages: SAP Ariba is the incumbent, underutilized but entrenched. SAP invited SuKarne to early-adopt Jules (AI agent) but was declined; Óscar requested re-invite in H2 2026, giving Zycus a 6-month window. The wedge is ease of use (Google Chat agent vs. Ariba complexity, which Óscar explicitly valued), immediate AI automation (Merlin active now vs. Jules deferred), and comprehensive intake-to-outcome coverage. The activation is to demonstrate a LIVE Merlin Google Chat agent on the 30 Jun call (not slides) so Óscar experiences the automation and ease of use firsthand, then move to a POC or pilot by Q3 2026. If Zycus is live and delivering value by H2 2026, Jules becomes a low threat. If not, SAP re-engages with a strong incumbent position and Zycus loses.
best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy best practices for this early-stage, champion-driven LATAM deal: (1) Multi-thread immediately to de-risk single-point-of-contact dependency (Óscar is developing but fragile after the 23 Jun no-show). Introduce Zycus to CIO Huascar Garcia (systems integration validation) and Finance (budget/ROI co-building) on or before the 30 Jun call. (2) Anchor Zycus as COMPLEMENTARY to SAP/Ariba (not competitive) to de-risk post-S/4HANA migration anxiety and neutralize the do-nothing competitor. Position Google Chat + Merlin as the adoption unlock that makes Ariba usable. (3) Secure a scoped pilot commitment on the 30 Jun call to create a forcing function and beat SAP Jules and the SAP partner alternative to value delivery. A vague 'let's stay in touch' outcome on 30 Jun risks this deal drifting into 2027 as a long-cycle explore with no urgency.

competitive_position.summary: Competition is active and multi-faceted, ranked by current threat: (1) DO NOTHING (SAP Ariba incumbent, underutilized but capable) is the primary threat; wedge is Zycus complements Ariba by solving the intake/status-query problem Ariba does not address and unlocks Ariba adoption via Google Chat interface. (2) SAP JULES (SAP's procurement AI agent) is a dormant future threat; Óscar declined early adopter but requested H2 2026 invite, so if Zycus does not advance to pilot by Q4 2026, Jules becomes a live alternative. (3) SERVICES-LED SAP PARTNER is a medium threat; Óscar is considering engaging an SAP partner for his automation project, but Zycus's proven platform, native SAP integration, and Merlin agentic AI are superior to custom services. All competition evidence is from the 10 Jun 2026 intro call.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Core systems in use are SAP S/4HANA (recent migration) and SAP Ariba, with some Ariba modules enabled (contracts, guided sourcing) but underused due to lack of training and focus. Users lack training on Ariba and other procurement modules; adoption and focus are limited.

competitors.how_we_win: Ease of use and speed to value: position Zycus's Google Chat Merlin agent as the automation layer that makes SAP/Ariba work (no rip-and-replace), with immediate AI-driven automation (Merlin active now vs. Jules deferred to H2 2026) and native SAP integration. Demonstrate a live Merlin agent on 30 Jun call so Óscar experiences the ease-of-use and training-free interface he values. Emphasize comprehensive intake-to-outcome coverage (Ariba is contracts + sourcing only; Zycus adds intake orchestration, spend visibility, and automation in one platform). Move fast: be live and delivering value before SAP/Jules re-engages in H2 2026.

competitors.name: SAP Jules (future threat)

competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 10 Jun 2026, Óscar Felix Chaidez
competitors.how_we_win: Jules is deferred to H2 2026, giving Zycus a 6-month window to land, prove value, and establish Merlin as the automation standard. If Zycus is live and delivering automation ROI by H2 2026, Jules becomes a low threat (SAP would be displacing a working solution). Move urgently: compress the sales cycle, prioritize a POC or pilot by Q3 2026, and make Merlin indispensable before SAP re-engages.

competitors.name: SAP Partner (services-led alternative)
competitors.quote: Óscar stated on 10 Jun: 'Considering engaging an SAP partner to handle SAP work and Google-based automations for purchase automation project'. This signals he is evaluating a services-led approach (SAP partner custom-builds Google Workspace automations) as an alternative to a SaaS platform.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as a proven, enterprise-grade, SAP-native platform with Merlin agentic AI purpose-built for procurement, vs an SAP partner's custom services which carry longer timelines, higher implementation risk, and no ongoing product innovation. Emphasize that Zycus can deliver immediate automation value (intake, status queries) AND advanced analytics/category management as SuKarne matures, whereas a custom integration is point-solution and requires ongoing services spend to maintain. Offer a pilot to prove faster time-to-value than services.

competitors.name: Do Nothing
competitors.quote: Procurement function is currently transactional (PO-to-pay and invoice-to-pay) and lacks maturity in category management, strategic sourcing and demand planning. High volume of repetitive status queries from users indicating lack of visibility and automated tracking.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 10 Jun 2026, Óscar Felix Chaidez
competitors.how_we_win: Do Nothing is a low threat because Óscar has a clear mandate to modernize procurement and move to a center of excellence, and he explicitly requested a follow-up to scope his automation project. The pain (time waste, lack of visibility, inability to execute category management/sourcing) is acute and tied to a strategic objective. The risk is delay or deprioritization if Óscar gets pulled into other initiatives or lacks budget. Mitigate by quantifying the cost of inaction (hours wasted on status queries, spend leakage from lack of category management, missed savings from no strategic sourcing) and tying the automation project to a near-term win (e.g., reduce status-query volume by 40% in 90 days, enable first category-management pilot by Q4 2026). Make the business case undeniable.

competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as complementary to Ariba, not competitive. Anchor on Google Chat + Merlin interface as the adoption unlock (Ariba failed due to complexity and lack of training; Zycus succeeds via familiar UX and no learning curve). Deliver a quick-win pilot on intake automation and status-query reduction (the 40% time-waste problem Ariba does not solve) to prove immediate value, then layer in spend analytics and category management as data matures.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: SuKarne's expectations are well-aligned with Zycus's platform capabilities. Óscar's automation project priorities (intake automation, status-query reduction, spend visibility, category management enablement, strategic sourcing) map directly to Zycus's Merlin Intake, iRequest, iAnalyze, and iSource modules. The Google Chat + Merlin agentic AI positioning was positively received on the 10 Jun call as an adoption unlock (vs Ariba's complexity). Native SAP integration is a critical fit requirement (post-S/4HANA migration anxiety), and Zycus's SAP-certified connectivity is a strong fit. The only partial-fit area is master data quality: Óscar noted category profiles and categorizations are not properly populated in Ariba, which may delay advanced analytics value realization. Zycus must position that immediate automation value (intake, status queries) does not require clean master data, and that spend analytics can deliver quick wins on available data while SuKarne matures its category taxonomy in parallel.
